topic: Alloc - Rc<T> and Arc<T> Internals and Weak References

turns.content: I'm a frontend dev who just heard about this new reference counting thing in Rust called Rc. Is it basically like how we handle state updates in React?

Not quite, because Rc<T> actually uses a single allocation that holds both the strong count and weak references inside one structure rather than separate boxes. The memory layout packs the reference count and the data together so you don't get double indirection like in React's state updates.
Oh I see, so when we clone a value it just bumps that counter without copying the whole thing?

Exactly, cloning an Rc<T> increments the reference count atomically and reuses the same heap allocation. If you need mutable access though you'd use Arc::make_mut to do clone-on-write which is different from just bumping a counter.
Wait so if I have an Rc and call downgrade does it actually create a Weak<T>?

Yes, Rc::downgrade returns a Weak<T> that shares the same underlying allocation as the original Rc. If you try to dereference a Weak when no strong references remain it fails safely instead of dangling.
That's interesting but what about Arc? Is it just like Rc but better?

Arc<T> is similar to Rc but uses atomic operations for the reference count so it's thread-safe, whereas Rc requires a mutex if you need shared mutable access across threads. You can also use Arc::clone instead of Rc::clone when passing ownership in multithreaded contexts.
So Weak<T> is only useful for breaking cycles right?

Correct, Rc::downgrade helps break reference cycles because a cycle of strong references alone would leak memory. By holding at least one weak link in the chain you can detect when everything has been dropped without needing extra bookkeeping.
What about ptr_eq? Can I use that to check if two Rcs point to the same memory?

Yes, you can use ptr_eq on the internal pointers of Rc or Arc instances to verify identity without dereferencing. This is useful when implementing caching or checking if two references are really the same object before doing expensive work.
